title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

traditional Oaxacan Black Mole step by step

seo.h1: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) Recipe
h2Headings: What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?
Ingredients for Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole
Expert Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole
seo.imageAlt: A rich, dark Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) served over chicken, garnished with sesame seeds and cilantro.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the deep, complex flavors of this traditional Oaxacan Black Mole, a culinary masterpiece perfect for your next Mexican feast.

description: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole is a majestic dish, a symphony of flavors from chiles, spices, nuts, and chocolate. This deeply rich and complex sauce, a true culinary treasure of Oaxaca, is often served with poultry and rice. Its dark hue and intricate taste tell a story of tradition and passion in every spoonful.

instructions.title: Prepare and Rehydrate Chiles
instructions.text: Lightly toast the dried chiles in a dry skillet over medium heat for about 30 seconds per side until fragrant. Be careful not to burn them. Transfer the toasted chiles to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft. Drain and reserve the rehydrating liquid.

instructions.title: Roast Vegetables and Toast Dry Ingredients
instructions.text: In a separate dry skillet, roast the tomatillos, tomatoes, onion, and unpeeled garlic cloves until softened and slightly charred. Set aside. In a pan with a tablespoon of lard or oil, toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds, raisins, and tortilla pieces until golden. Remove and set aside. In the same pan, lightly toast the cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, cumin, anise, oregano, and thyme until aromatic. Combine all toasted dry ingredients and roasted vegetables.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Combine the rehydrated chiles, roasted vegetables, toasted nuts, seeds, spices, and tortilla pieces in a blender. Add about 2 cups of the warm chicken broth and blend until a very smooth paste forms. This may require blending in batches and adding more broth or reserved chile liquid as needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency. Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl, pressing with a spoon to extract all liquid and discard solids. This step is crucial for a smooth mole.

instructions.title: Simmer the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, heat the remaining lard or vegetable oil over medium heat. Carefully pour in the strained mole paste. It will splatter, so be cautious. Cook, stirring constantly, for at least 15-20 minutes until the mole darkens in color and thickens considerably, becoming very fragrant and shiny. This step is essential for developing the deep flavors.

instructions.title: Add Liquids, Chocolate, and Sweetener
instructions.text: Gradually add the remaining warm chicken broth to the mole paste, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Bring the mole to a gentle simmer. Stir in the chopped Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (or brown sugar) until fully melted and incorporated. Continue to simmer the mole, stirring occasionally, for at least 1 to 1.5 hours, or even longer, until the sauce is very thick, rich, and the flavors have fully melded. Season with salt to taste. The mole should have a deep, complex flavor profile, balancing savory, sweet, and spicy notes.

instructions.title: Serve Your Authentic Mole Negro
instructions.text: Serve the Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole warm over cooked chicken pieces, turkey, or pork. It is traditionally accompanied by white rice and warm tortillas. Garnish with a sprinkle of sesame seeds and fresh cilantro if desired. This mole is a labor of love, meant to be savored.

tips: For the deepest flavor, ensure your chiles are perfectly toasted, not burnt. A slight char adds complexity, but too much bitterness will ruin the mole.
Always use a good quality Mexican chocolate, preferably one with a hint of cinnamon and almonds, to achieve the authentic taste of Oaxacan Mole Negro.
Mole Negro tastes even better the next day. Make it ahead of time, allow the flavors to meld, and gently reheat before serving for an enhanced experience.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Oaxaca, Mexico, where a dish of unparalleled depth and complexity reigns supreme: Mole Negro. This isn't just a sauce; it's a symphony of flavors, a testament to tradition, and a beloved Mexican dinner recipe that transforms any meal into a special occasion. Often considered the "king" of moles, Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) is a masterpiece of balance, weaving together an astonishing array of ingredients to create a rich, smoky, and subtly sweet experience that lingers on the palate. If you're ready to dive into the art of authentic Mexican cooking, preparing this iconic dish is a profoundly rewarding adventure.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?

Oaxacan Black Mole, or Mole Negro, is arguably the most famous and intricate of Oaxaca's "seven moles," a collection of distinct sauces that define the region's gastronomic identity. Far from a simple sauce, Mole Negro is a culinary monument, often containing 20 to 30 or even more ingredients, including multiple varieties of chiles, nuts, seeds, spices, fruits, vegetables, and a crucial touch of dark chocolate. Its characteristic deep, almost black color comes from the careful roasting and charring of the chiles, particularly the chilhuacle negro, which is endemic to Oaxaca and essential for an authentic flavor profile. The preparation of this mole is a labor of love, traditionally taking hours, if not days, to achieve its signature velvety texture and profound flavor. It’s a dish reserved for celebrations, holidays, and family gatherings, symbolizing heritage and the rich culinary history of Mexico.

## Ingredients for Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole

Creating an authentic Oaxacan Black Mole requires a commitment to sourcing specific ingredients, many of which contribute to its unique flavor and color. While some substitutions can be made, aiming for the real deal will yield the most rewarding experience.

**Essential Chiles (dried):**
*   6-8 Chilhuacle Negro chiles (crucial for authentic flavor and color; often found in specialty Mexican markets)
*   4-6 Mulato chiles
*   4-6 Pasilla chiles
*   2-3 Ancho chiles
*   (Optional: 1-2 Chipotle chiles for a smoky kick)

**Aromatics & Vegetables:**
*   1 large white onion, roughly chopped
*   6-8 cloves garlic, peeled
*   2 ripe tomatoes, roughly chopped
*   ½ plantain, sliced
*   ½ cup raisins

**Nuts & Seeds:**
*   ¼ cup blanched almonds
*   ¼ cup peanuts
*   2 tablespoons sesame seeds
*   1 tablespoon pumpkin seeds (pepitas)

**Spices (whole preferred, then ground):**
*   1 teaspoon whole black peppercorns
*   ½ teaspoon whole cloves
*   ½ teaspoon cumin seeds
*   2-3 small pieces of canela (Mexican cinnamon stick)
*   2-3 sprigs fresh thyme or ½ teaspoon dried
*   2-3 bay leaves
*   Pinch of dried oregano

**Thickeners & Flavor Enhancers:**
*   2 slices stale bread or 2 corn tortillas, torn
*   2-3 tablespoons lard or vegetable oil
*   5-6 cups chicken broth (or vegetable broth for a vegetarian option)
*   ½ tablet (about 1.5 oz) Mexican chocolate (e.g., Ibarra or Abuelita), grated
*   1-2 tablespoons piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone), grated, or dark brown sugar, to taste
*   Salt to taste

**Protein:**
*   1 whole chicken, cut into pieces, or 3-4 lbs pork shoulder, slow-cooked until tender

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Mole Negro

Preparing Oaxacan Black Mole is a multi-stage process, but each step builds upon the last, creating layers of flavor. Patience is your most important ingredient.

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully remove stems and seeds from all dried chiles. Open them flat. Heat a comal or dry skillet over medium-low heat. Toast the chiles one by one, pressing them down with tongs, for about 15-30 seconds per side until fragrant but not burnt (burnt chiles will make the mole bitter). This step is crucial for the dark color of Oaxacan Black Mole. Immediately transfer toasted chiles to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for at least 30 minutes until soft.
2.  **Roast Aromatics & Spices:** In the same comal or skillet, toast the sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds, almonds, and peanuts separately until golden and fragrant. Set aside. Next, lightly char the onion, garlic, and tomatoes until softened and slightly blackened.
3.  **Sauté Other Ingredients:** In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat 1 tablespoon of lard or oil. Sauté the plantain slices until lightly browned. Add the raisins and sauté briefly until plump. Add the stale bread or tortillas and fry until crispy.
4.  **Grind the Mole Paste:** Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving some of the soaking liquid. In batches, blend the chiles, roasted onion, garlic, tomatoes, plantain, raisins, nuts, seeds, and all whole spices (peppercorns, cloves, cumin, cinnamon, thyme, bay leaves, oregano) with a little chicken broth and reserved chile soaking liquid until you achieve a very smooth paste. This may require multiple blending cycles and pushing the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve if your blender isn't super powerful. The smoother the paste, the better the mole.
5.  **Cook the Mole:** In the same large pot or Dutch oven (cleaned if necessary), heat the remaining lard or oil over medium heat. Carefully add the mole paste. This step is critical: you must fry the mole paste, stirring constantly, for at least 30-45 minutes. It will splatter, so use a splatter guard. This frying process deepens the flavor and darkens the color of the Oaxacan Black Mole. The paste will thicken and become very fragrant.
6.  **Simmer and Season:** Gradually add the chicken broth, stirring continuously to prevent lumps, until the mole reaches your desired consistency (it should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon). Add the grated Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (or brown sugar). Stir until dissolved. Bring to a gentle simmer, then reduce heat to low, cover, and cook for at least 1-2 hours, stirring occasionally. The longer it simmers, the more the flavors meld.
7.  **Adjust and Serve:** Taste the mole and adjust seasoning with salt, sugar, or a touch more chocolate if needed. The flavor should be complex, balanced between smoky, spicy, and subtly sweet. If serving with chicken or pork, add the cooked protein to the mole during the last 30 minutes of simmering to allow it to absorb the flavors.

## Expert Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole

*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** This is the most critical step. Burnt chiles will impart a bitter taste to your Oaxacan Black Mole that cannot be corrected. Toast them gently until fragrant, not smoky or charred beyond recognition.
*   **Charring is Key:** For the aromatics (onion, garlic, tomato), a good char adds depth and a beautiful dark hue to the mole.
*   **Blend Until Silky Smooth:** A traditional molino (grinding stone) produces the finest texture. If using a blender, blend in small batches and be patient. For the smoothest results, press the blended paste through a fine-mesh sieve.
*   **Fry the Paste Thoroughly:** This step, often called "sazonar," is non-negotiable. Frying the mole paste in lard or oil for an extended period develops its flavor, deepens its color, and changes its texture. Don't rush it!
*   **Patience with Simmering:** Mole is not a fast dish. The long, slow simmer allows all the complex flavors to marry and develop.
*   **Resting Improves Flavor:** Like a good stew, Oaxacan Black Mole often tastes even better the next day after the flavors have had more time to meld.

## Serving Suggestions for Your Mole

Oaxacan Black Mole is traditionally served with chicken or turkey, but it's equally delicious with pork.
*   **Classic Platter:** Ladle generous amounts of mole over cooked chicken or pork pieces.
*   **Rice and Tortillas:** Always serve with fluffy white rice to soak up the glorious sauce and warm corn tortillas for scooping.
*   **Garnish:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, chopped fresh cilantro, or thinly sliced red onion adds a lovely finish.
*   **Tamales:** Mole Negro makes an incredible filling for tamales.
*   **Enmoladas:** Use the mole to dip tortillas, fill them with cheese or chicken, roll them up, and bathe them in more mole. A fantastic Mexican dinner recipe idea!

## Variations and Substitutions

While authentic Oaxacan Black Mole relies on specific ingredients, some adjustments can be made:
*   **Chiles:** If chilhuacle negro chiles are unavailable, a mix of mulato, pasilla, and ancho chiles can still yield a delicious dark mole, though the flavor profile will be slightly different. You might also add a small amount of dark cocoa powder (unsweetened) to enhance the color.
*   **Protein:** While chicken and pork are traditional, you can make a vegetarian version using mushrooms, sweet potatoes, or plantains, and vegetable broth instead of chicken broth.
*   **Sweetener:** Piloncillo is best, but dark brown sugar or even a touch of agave syrup can work.
*   **Fat:** Lard imparts a wonderful flavor, but vegetable oil is a suitable substitute.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: Can I make Oaxacan Black Mole ahead of time?**
A: Absolutely! Mole is an ideal make-ahead dish. In fact, many believe the flavors deepen and improve after a day or two in the refrigerator.

**Q: Can Mole Negro be frozen?**
A: Yes, it freezes beautifully. Once cooled, store it in airtight containers or freezer bags for up to 3-4 months. Thaw in the refrigerator overnight and reheat gently on the stovetop, adding a splash of broth if it's too thick.

**Q: What if my mole is too bitter?**
A: Bitterness usually comes from burnt chiles. If it's only slightly bitter, you can try adding a bit more piloncillo/sugar or Mexican chocolate, or even a small amount of peanut butter to mellow it out. Unfortunately, if the chiles were severely burnt, it might be hard to salvage.

**Q: Do I really need all those different chiles?**
A: For an authentic Oaxacan Black Mole, yes, the blend of chiles is crucial. Each chile contributes a unique flavor profile – smoky, fruity, earthy, mild heat – that creates the complex tapestry of the mole. Using fewer varieties will still result in a delicious sauce, but it won't have the same depth as a traditional Mole Negro.

Creating Oaxacan Black Mole is an act of culinary devotion, a journey into the soul of Mexican cooking. It requires time, patience, and a willingness to embrace a multitude of ingredients, but the reward is an unforgettable dining experience. This rich, complex, and utterly delicious Mexican dinner recipe will undoubtedly become a cherished addition to your kitchen repertoire.
